title: Folding Tray / Lap Desk
summary: A breakfast-in-bed tray with bolt-pivot folding legs — a 1x2 frame, smooth plywood top, and a 15° foot bevel that doubles as the finger pull. A classic gift build.
description:
The folding bed tray is a gift-shop staple that costs three boards to build: a 1x2 frame under a smooth 1/4" plywood top, with two U-shaped leg sets that pivot flat on 1/4" bolts. Breakfast in bed, laptop desk, couch dinner tray — and it stores flat behind the nightstand between uses.

The legs carry the plan's two smart cuts. The tops of the leg uprights are pointed at 45° so they can rotate past the frame as they fold — a square top jams against the tray on the way around. And the bottom corner takes a 15° miter, which the source credits with two jobs: the angled foot sits flatter on a bedspread when the legs are open (they splay slightly), and the cut corner becomes the FINGER PULL you grab to unfold the legs. One small miter, two functions — the kind of detail worth stealing for other builds.

The pivot stack is bolt / frame / leg / washer / nut. Snug the nuts until the legs swing with friction but hold their position; nylon lock nuts hold that adjustment forever, plain nuts need a re-snug every few months.

Use SMOOTH plywood for the top (sande ply, as sourced, or any clean-faced 1/4") and finish with a wipeable topcoat — this surface will meet coffee, syrup, and watercolors, possibly the same morning. Round every corner and edge generously; it's furniture that lives in laps.

tools.note: The 45° leg points and 15° foot bevels.
The 1/4" pivot bolt holes — drilled straight, or the legs splay unevenly.
1-1/4" brads assemble the frame; screws or pocket holes work too.
Round everything. This lives in laps.

materials.name: 1x2, 12 linear ft
materials.note: Frame and legs. Pick clear, straight stock — thin parts show every knot.

materials.name: Sande plywood, 1/4", 24" x 13-1/2"
materials.note: The top. Any smooth-faced 1/4" ply works.

materials.name: Bolts, 1/4" x 2-1/2", with washers and nuts
materials.note: Nylon lock nuts hold the pivot friction where you set it.

materials.name: Paint or stain plus clear topcoat

cutList.note: The pivot bolt holes drill through these.
Top cut to a 45° point (clears the frame in the fold), bottom at 15° (flat foot + finger pull).

steps.title: Drill the pivots, build the frame
steps.body:
Mark the bolt positions on the 24" frame sides and drill the 1/4" holes — straight through, square to the face; a crooked pivot hole becomes a leg that splays its own direction. Then assemble the frame rectangle with glue and brads (screws or pocket holes if you prefer).

Don't fuss over frame strength — the plywood top, once glued on, turns the frame into a rigid panel.

steps.title: Build the leg sets
steps.body:
Cut the four uprights — 45° point at the top, 15° miter at the bottom corner — and join each pair with a cross rail into a U. Drill the 1/4" pivot holes through the pointed tops, matching the frame's hole spacing exactly (stack-drill frame and leg together if you can clamp them aligned).

The 15° foot miter is the double-duty cut: flatter footing on soft surfaces when the legs splay open, and the cut corner is the finger pull for unfolding. Small cut, keep it.

steps.title: Bolt the legs on
steps.body: Stack each pivot: bolt head outside the frame, through the frame side, through the leg, washer, nut inside. Snug until the legs swing smoothly but hold position — then fold and unfold both sets a dozen times and re-check. Lock nuts keep the setting; plain nuts will want seasonal attention.
materials: Bolts, 1/4" x 2-1/2", with washers and nuts

steps.title: Top, finish, bow
steps.body:
Glue and nail the plywood top to the frame, smooth face up. Sand every edge and corner to lap-friendly round, then paint or stain and seal with a wipeable clear coat.

Personalize if it's a gift — a monogram, a painted border — and, per the source's closing instruction, don't forget the pretty bow.
materials: Paint or stain plus clear topcoat

images.alt: Folding wooden bed tray with pivoting legs
